{"content":"# Metadata
## Title: Every Folder on My Computer (Full Reveal)
## Author: Tiago Forte
## Author URL: https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCmvYCRYPDlzSHVNCI_ViJDQ
## Thumbnail: https://i.ytimg.com/vi_webp/A0pdL3MS_7E/sddefault.webp

# Transcript
[0.0s] I'm going to do something I don't
[1.76s] normally do. I'm going to show you every
[3.929s] [music] folder on my computer. My real
[7.36s] setup across my laptop plus three
[10.2s] separate drives. It's actually simpler
[12.445s] [music] than you'd think and that's the
[13.92s] whole point. I wrote a whole book about
[15.96s] it, two books actually, but don't worry
[17.88s] about the name. Just watch what it lets
[20.08s] me do. I can point AI at any [music]
[23.0s] part of my work or my life in just a
[25.44s] click or two. By the way, that's PARA.
[28.92s] Four folders [music] that I've taught
[30.76s] for over a decade now.
[33.96s] I want to show you how I've set up the
[36.025s] [music] shortcuts to my PARA folders in
[38.92s] the sidebar of my finder. [music] And
[40.84s] you can see there's kind of a lot of
[42.04s] things over here on the left, but they
[44.28s] come in a very specific order in four
[47.0s] separate parts. These first three are
[50.28s] local folders on my computer. This is my
[52.68s] home folder which contains everything
[54.72s] for my user on this computer, my desktop
[58.0s] and my downloads. Some things exist only
[60.68s] on my computer and don't really need to
[62.72s] go anywhere else and that's those three.
[64.64s] Next, I have my personal Google Drive.
[68.08s] So this is nothing related to work, just
[70.44s] my personal Google Drive attached to my
[73.36s] personal Gmail account which I've had
[75.16s] for 20 years. So you can see here what's
[77.52s] called My Drive contains an inbox plus
[80.68s] the four PARA folders. And this is all
[82.64s] personal stuff, so my personal projects
[85.52s] that you can see there, my personal
[87.52s] areas, my personal resources, and my
[90.48s] personal archives. I really like to keep
[92.76s] my personal life completely separate
[94.56s] from my work life. I also have each of
[96.36s] those five top-level folders
[98.76s] listed right down here as shortcuts. So
[101.64s] with a single click, I can jump into any
[104.24s] of the four categories of PARA. Then,
[106.48s] just below I have the same thing but for
[108.64s] my individual work Google Drive. So this
[111.56s] is everything related to work but just
[113.24s] for my individual account that no one
[115.76s] else at the company has access to. So,
[117.44s] you can see here it's the same five
[119.0s] top-level folders, which I also have
[121.24s] here as shortcuts. My work projects, my
[123.68s] work areas, my work resources, and my
[126.44s] work archives. Finally, we have the
[128.92s] Forté [snorts] Labs share drive. So,
[130.679s] this is a share drive in that everyone
[132.959s] across the entire company has access to
[134.959s] it. Which, of course, we need for all of
[137.8s] our shared company projects, all of our
[140.2s] shared areas, all of our shared
[142.959s] resources, and all of our shared
[145.12s] archives. Now, you'll notice for the
[146.36s] Forté Labs share drive, I don't have the
[148.959s] full contents mirrored onto my local
[152.04s] machine as I do with my individual work
[155.92s] drive and my personal drive. And the
[158.2s] reason for that is the Forté Labs share
[160.08s] drive is something like 17 TB.
[162.48s] It's far too much data for me to store
[164.88s] on my computer. What I do in this case
[167.16s] is not mirroring, it's the other mode
[169.56s] that Google Drive offers, which is
[171.36s] streaming. With streaming, all of the
[173.64s] different folders that are on our share
[175.52s] drive appear here as if they are local
[178.2s] folders. But, you'll see here on the
[180.0s] right there's a little cloud symbol,
[181.88s] which is telling me that this folder
[183.84s] only lives in the cloud. It's not
[185.48s] actually on my computer, just a pointer
[187.959s] to a folder on Google servers. So, if I
[190.44s] have a folder that I want to work with
[191.959s] within Co-work, I'll have to right click
[194.36s] it and then click make available
[196.92s] offline. It will download this folder
[198.8s] onto my computer. The only folders from
[200.84s] the share drive that I have currently
[203.16s] downloaded are the two projects that I'm
[205.36s] most actively working on, which are my
[207.32s] book. You can see the the little green
[209.12s] check mark indicating that these are all
[210.76s] downloaded. And then, our program the AI
[212.959s] Second Brain, which is also downloaded
[215.4s] here. What you'll notice is that every
[217.48s] single project is actually part of a
[221.28s] broader area. So, if I'm working on my
[223.84s] life in perspective book, that is just
[226.6s] one single project within a much broader
[229.72s] area called books. Another project we're
[232.519s] doing is we're building a studio, a
[234.84s] recording studio in the town in Mexico
[237.2s] where I live. Well, that is not just one
[239.32s] little island in isolation. That is part
[241.68s] of an area at our company, which of
[243.52s] course is YouTube. So, what these
[245.72s] shortcuts give me
[247.28s] is instant one or two click access to
[250.52s] practically any file or folder that I
[253.32s] might want to point Co-worker at. And
[256.32s] the most powerful part is when you point
[258.72s] it at a whole project plus the bigger
[261.88s] area that it lives in. That's where it
[264.32s] really starts saving you a ton of time.
[266.84s] So, I'll go ahead and start a new task.
[268.48s] I'll say work in a project, choose a
[270.28s] different folder, and I will first give
[272.48s] it
[274.36s] the AI course folder, so it has all the
[276.48s] contents of this specific project. But
[278.8s] then I also I can click again, I also
[281.6s] want to choose a different folder
[283.6s] and select within areas my marketing
[287.96s] folder as well as my content. So, our
[290.68s] content is the main way that we do
[292.36s] marketing, so I want it to work across
[294.04s] both content and marketing
[296.24s] and hit open.
[298.2s] And always allow. So, you can see here
[300.04s] it now says AI course plus two. So, now
[302.28s] I can say analyze the contents of the AI
[305.04s] course folder. Then create a launch and
[308.4s] marketing plan and draw on the contents
[311.919s] of two area folders that I'm also giving
[314.6s] you. Suggest any ideas, possibilities,
[318.919s] new strategies or tactics that we could
[321.36s] use to reach our audience and new
[323.76s] audiences based on the long-term
[327.16s] learnings from those two area folders.
[329.56s] It's always going to start by scanning
[331.28s] the folders that you've given it to
[332.84s] understand what it's working with. So,
[334.24s] notice that it's reading the the
[335.6s] testimonials of our first cohort that we
[337.88s] just finished as well as the feedback
[340.2s] survey data. This is why it's so
[341.84s] important to have the current very
[344.44s] specific concrete project, which tends
[347.12s] to have very detailed data, as well as
[350.72s] the long-term broad themes that are are
[354.0s] kind of like the big picture view. Both
[355.92s] of those levels of detail, those levels
[358.68s] of abstraction, are very important. So,
[361.16s] this is super interesting. I can already
[362.88s] tell, even by this very high-level
[365.8s] summary, that it is drawing on just a a
[369.16s] bigger picture, a wider view of what
[372.28s] we're trying to accomplish through this
[374.48s] program beyond just this one single
[376.88s] launch that is happening over the course
[378.96s] of a few weeks. So, zooming out for a
[381.12s] second, do you guys understand the
[382.8s] implications of what we're seeing here?
[384.96s] There's a lot of other combinations that
[386.84s] you can use, especially if you use my
[389.44s] PARA method. For example, you could give
[391.88s] it two separate projects [music] and
[394.12s] say, "Compare and contrast these
[396.24s] projects. What are the strengths? What
[397.919s] are the weaknesses? How could they
[399.24s] support each other? How could the
[400.72s] learnings from one inform the other?"
[402.8s] You could give it two separate areas.
[405.16s] can lessons from my health inform my
[408.36s] work? How can ideas from [music]
[411.28s] my gardening inform my marketing?" You
[413.76s] could give it two separate resources,
[415.8s] even. [music] "How can I use my
[418.6s] knowledge of UX design [music] to, I
[421.36s] don't know, design more engaging slide
[423.68s] presentations that people pay more
[425.88s] attention to?" The possibilities are
[427.76s] really endless. [music] You could give
[428.96s] it, you know, all of your project
[431.0s] folders and ask it for an analysis of
[433.84s] your whole project portfolio. Which ones
[436.12s] are likely to succeed? [music]
[438.2s] Which ones are likely to fail? Which
[440.12s] ones you should consider
[441.475s] >> [music]
[441.64s] >> scoping downward or potentially even
[443.72s] canceling? You could give it your full
[445.6s] archive and ask it, "What are some past
[448.04s] things that I have undertaken that could
[450.16s] be revived or could be revisited or
[453.0s] could flow into current efforts?"
[454.575s] [music] Really, as long as you have
[456.32s] these pre-formed chunks of collections
[459.64s] of files, which is [music] really just a
[461.76s] folder hierarchy, which you can organize
[464.16s] using PARA or any other method,
[466.365s] >> [music]
[466.44s] >> then the world is your oyster. You can
[468.72s] mix and match and compare and combine
[471.12s] and merge and synthesize [music]
[472.88s] and split apart. It becomes this
[474.76s] playground of just melding and [music]
[478.28s] and sort of recombining different
[481.0s] groupings of content that probably
[484.28s] already exist on your computer in your
[487.16s] file system
[487.98s] >> [music]
[488.12s] >> as we speak. And here's the part that I
[490.6s] am most excited to share with you.
[492.919s] The way that you build this for yourself
[494.53s] [music] is a completely free Claude
[497.68s] skill that you can just load up right
[500.12s] into your LLM. You point it at your
[501.88s] files, it looks at everything you've
[503.68s] got, and then it will propose [music] a
[505.56s] step-by-step plan to reorganize all of
[508.56s] it into this exact system. We originally
[511.96s] built this skill [music] just for the
[513.56s] first cohort of our live training
[515.88s] program, the AI Second Brain. It was
[518.64s] only [music] for our students. Starting
[520.599s] today, it's free for anyone. Join my
[523.44s] newsletter and it's yours. The link is
[525.68s] in the description."}
